title: Chunking
lesson:
Imagine you hand a friend a 400-page book and ask, "Where does it talk about returning a faulty product?" Handing over the whole book is useless — they'd have to read all of it. What you really want is to slice the book into small, sensible pieces ahead of time, so you can grab just the right slice when asked.

That slicing is chunking: cutting a long document into smaller pieces (chunks) before you store and search them.

Why chunking decides if RAG works

Chunking is the unglamorous step that decides whether RAG (feeding a model your own documents) actually works. The flow is simple:

  • Turn each chunk into an embedding (a list of numbers that captures meaning).
  • Store those embeddings in a vector database.
  • At question time, fetch the few chunks closest to the user's query.

If your chunks are bad, retrieval is bad — no clever model downstream can fix it.

Why size is everything

Think of chunking like cutting a pizza. One giant uncut pizza is awkward to share. But slice it into a hundred crumbs and nobody gets a real bite. You want slices big enough to satisfy, small enough to hand out cleanly. Chunks work the same way.

Make chunks too big and two problems appear:

  • One chunk covers several topics, so its embedding becomes a blurry average — like one map pin planted between three cities, near none of them.
  • You waste your context window (the limited text a model can read at once) stuffing in mostly-irrelevant text.

Make chunks too small and each loses its context: a sentence reading "It costs $9 a month" is useless if the chunk before it — naming the product — got cut away.

Sensible defaults and overlap

A common starting point in 2026 is roughly 200–500 tokens per chunk (a token is a small chunk of text, often a word-piece), with a small overlap between neighbours. Overlap means each chunk repeats the last sentence or two of the previous one.

Why? Picture tearing a long strip of paper into pieces — a sentence that straddles a tear gets ripped in half and its meaning is lost. Overlap is like photocopying the torn edges onto both pieces, so no idea falls down the crack. A typical overlap is 10–20% of the chunk size.

Smarter than fixed sizes

  • Fixed-size chunking — cut every N characters or tokens, no matter what. Fast, but it happily slices mid-sentence.
  • Recursive chunking — try to split on big boundaries first (paragraphs), and only if a piece is still too long, fall back to sentences, then words. This respects the document's natural seams.
  • Semantic chunking — embed each sentence and start a new chunk whenever the topic visibly shifts, so each chunk holds one coherent idea. Smartest, but slowest.
Fixed-sizeSemantic / recursive
Cut every N tokens, blindlyCut on meaning or structure boundaries
Fast and dead simpleSlower, needs more logic
Often splits mid-thoughtKeeps each idea intact
Fine for uniform textBetter for mixed, structured docs

Here is recursive chunking with overlap, the workhorse for most projects:

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter

splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=400,        # target size (in characters here)
    chunk_overlap=60,      # repeat ~15% into the next chunk
    separators=["\n\n", "\n", ". ", " "],  # try big seams first
)
chunks = splitter.split_text(open("handbook.txt").read())
print(len(chunks), "chunks")
print(chunks[0][:120])

One detail people miss: store useful metadata (extra labels) alongside each chunk — its source file, page number, section title. When you later retrieve a chunk, you can show "from Handbook p.12" and your retrieval pipeline can filter by it.

Match the chunk to the document

There is no universal best size. Code wants to be split on function boundaries, not every 400 characters. A chat log splits naturally per message. A legal contract splits per clause. Markdown splits on headings. The trick is to honour whatever structure already exists.

A short worked example: take a product FAQ where each entry is a question and answer. Chunking by FAQ entry (one Q+A per chunk) almost always beats fixed-size, because each chunk is already a self-contained, askable unit — exactly the shape a user's question will match.

💡 Always re-chunk and re-embed together. If you change your chunking strategy, the old embeddings no longer match the new chunks — rebuild the index from scratch.

Get chunking right and the rest of RAG falls into place. Get it wrong and you'll blame the model for answers that were doomed the moment you sliced the text.
mentalModel: Chunking is slicing a big book into bite-sized pages before filing them, so that later you can pull out exactly the right page — not the whole book, and not a meaningless scrap.
takeaways: Chunking slices long documents into small pieces before embedding and storing them.
Too big = blurry embeddings; too small = lost context. Add overlap.
Split on natural structure, not blindly every N tokens.

diagram.caption: A long document is sliced into overlapping chunks, each embedded into a vector database, so a query can fetch the single best-matching slice.

quiz.q: You ask a RAG system a question and it returns chunks that are vaguely on-topic but never quite answer it. The chunks are very large. What is the most likely cause?
options: Each big chunk covers many topics, so its embedding is a blurry average that matches no specific query well
The embeddings are too precise and overfit to single words
The vector database is too small to store the chunks
The model's context window is too large
quiz.explain: Oversized chunks mix multiple ideas, so the embedding averages them and lands near nothing in particular. Smaller, single-idea chunks produce sharper matches.

quiz.q: Why do most chunking setups add a small overlap (e.g. 10–20%) between neighbouring chunks?
options: To make the vector database run faster
So a sentence or idea split across a chunk boundary still appears whole in at least one chunk
To reduce the total number of chunks created
Because embeddings require duplicate text to work
quiz.explain: Overlap photocopies the torn edges onto both neighbours, so meaning straddling a boundary isn't lost. It actually increases chunk count slightly, not reduces it.

quiz.q: You are chunking a product FAQ where each entry is one question plus its answer. Which strategy will most reliably give self-contained, retrievable chunks?
options: Fixed-size cuts every 200 characters regardless of structure
One chunk per character so nothing is ever too big
One chunk per FAQ entry (each Q+A as a unit)
Put the entire FAQ into a single chunk
quiz.explain: Each Q+A is already a complete, askable unit that matches the shape of a user's question. Fixed-size cuts would split questions from answers, and one giant chunk blurs everything together.

quiz.q: You improve your pipeline by switching from fixed-size to recursive chunking, but reuse the embeddings already in your vector database. Retrieval gets worse. Why?
options: Recursive chunking is always worse than fixed-size
The old embeddings were built from the old chunks and no longer match the new ones, so the index is corrupted
Recursive chunking can't be embedded by standard models
The context window shrank when you changed strategies
quiz.explain: Embeddings are tied to the exact chunks they were made from. Change the chunking and you must re-embed and rebuild the index, or stored vectors point at text that no longer exists.

mistakes: Chunks too big. One chunk covering many topics produces a blurry, average embedding that matches no query well, and wastes context-window space on irrelevant text. Aim for one main idea per chunk.

Chunks too small. A lone sentence often loses the context that makes it meaningful ("It costs $9" — what does?). Tiny chunks scatter related facts across many pieces, so retrieval misses half the answer.
No overlap. Splitting with zero overlap rips sentences and ideas in half at chunk boundaries. A small 10–20% overlap photocopies the torn edges onto both neighbours so meaning isn't lost.

Ignoring the document's natural structure. Blindly cutting code, tables, or FAQs every N tokens shreds units that were already self-contained. Split on functions, rows, headings, or Q+A pairs instead.
Forgetting metadata. Storing bare text with no source, page, or section means you can't cite where an answer came from or filter retrieval by document. Attach labels to every chunk.
Changing chunking without re-embedding. New chunks and old embeddings no longer line up, silently corrupting your index. Whenever you change chunk size or strategy, rebuild and re-embed everything.
exercise:
Take a real document you have — a long blog post, a product manual, or a meeting transcript saved as a text file. Write a script that splits it three ways: (1) fixed-size at 200 characters with no overlap, (2) fixed-size at 200 with 40-character overlap, and (3) recursive splitting on paragraphs then sentences (the RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter shown above). Print how many chunks each produces and eyeball the first three chunks of each. Notice where method (1) cuts mid-sentence and method (3) does not.

Stretch goal: Embed all chunks from method (1) and method (3) with the same embedding model, ask one realistic question, and retrieve the top chunk from each set by cosine similarity. Compare which method returned the more complete, self-contained answer — and write one sentence explaining why.
solution: You should see fixed-size (method 1) produce the most chunks and visibly chop sentences in half — words and even thoughts get severed at the 200-character mark. Method 2 looks similar but the repeated text at each boundary means a sentence split across two chunks now appears whole in at least one of them. Method 3 produces fewer, cleaner chunks that end on natural breaks like paragraph or sentence ends. For the stretch goal, the recursive chunks usually win retrieval: because each one holds a complete idea, the top match reads as a coherent answer, while the fixed-size top match may start or end mid-thought and miss the surrounding context the user needed. The lesson: where you cut matters as much as how big the pieces are.
summary: Chunking slices long documents into small pieces before you embed and store them for RAG. Size is the key dial: too big gives blurry embeddings and wasted context, too small loses meaning. Use a small overlap so ideas aren't cut in half, split on the document's natural structure (paragraphs, headings, functions, Q+A) rather than blindly, and attach metadata. Recursive or semantic chunking usually beats fixed-size. Change your chunking and you must re-embed.

keyTerms.def: A small piece of a larger document, created so it can be embedded, stored, and retrieved on its own.
The process of cutting a long document into smaller pieces before storing and searching them, usually for RAG.
How big each chunk is, measured in tokens or characters. A common starting point is roughly 200–500 tokens.
Repeating the last sentence or two of one chunk at the start of the next, so ideas straddling a boundary aren't lost.
The simplest method: cut every N tokens or characters regardless of meaning. Fast but often splits mid-sentence.
Splitting on large boundaries first (paragraphs), then smaller ones (sentences, words) only when a piece is still too long, to respect natural seams.
Starting a new chunk wherever the topic shifts, detected by comparing sentence embeddings, so each chunk holds one coherent idea.
Extra labels stored with each chunk (source file, page, section) used for citing answers and filtering retrieval.
